summary: The post argues that security mechanisms create “affordances” and that strict, binary enforcement can erase legitimate, human exceptions. Ronacher compares encryption to ticket gates: it works by blocking access, but that rigidity creates social, legal, and operational downsides. He contends that the cost of universal SSL is higher than commonly acknowledged, spanning trust infrastructure, CPU costs, deployment complexity, and a false sense of safety. He warns that ubiquitous encryption shifts power and incentives, encouraging MITM practices and complicating lawful interception and emergency access. The conclusion is not anti-encryption, but a call for selective, context-aware use and a broader societal discussion about the tradeoffs of making cryptography the default.
key_points: Binary enforcement removes legitimate exceptions; encryption, like gates, has no humane override.
The SSL trust model and CA system do not scale cleanly and dilute the meaning of “secure.”
Operational and economic costs of SSL (CPU, IPv4/SNI limitations, certificate sprawl) are real and often hidden.
Widespread encryption incentivizes MITM tools and normalizes unsafe user behavior around certificate warnings.
Complexity and misuse risks in TLS/SSL make universal deployment a safety liability for average administrators.
money_quotes: Encryption is like a ticket gate in the sense, that there is no way around it.
SSL is bloody expensive compared to not doing it.
The cost of deploying SSL should not be underestimated, and forcing SSL on people out of principle should consider that.
I'm firmly of the opinion that none of that would have happened if SSL traffic was less common.
There are probably more misconfigured SSL systems that give users the illusion of safety than correctly set up ones.
Encryption is a good thing, but I believe it needs to be applied carefully.
key_insight: Universal encryption is a blunt, binary tool whose social and operational side effects are under-discussed, so it should be applied selectively rather than by default.
